OT79Y9UAAAAJ.keywords: Networking, Networked Services, Wireless and Mobile Networks, Multicast, Multimedia Communication, Delay-Tolerant Networks, Message Ferrying, Network Virtualization, Network Function Virtualization (NFV), Mobile Edge Computing
OT79Y9UAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mostafa Ammar is a **Regents' Professor** and **Interim Chair** in the *School of Computer Science* at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a long-standing research program in Computer Networking and Networked Systems (see his lab page: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ammar). His work spans design, analysis, and systems-building across wired and wireless environments with a sustained emphasis on <u>robust communication in partitioned and heterogeneous networks</u>, <u>scalable content and service distribution</u>, and <u>resource virtualization and orchestration</u>. He is particularly known for foundational contributions to *delay-tolerant networking (DTN)* and *message ferrying*, scalable *multicast and multimedia delivery*, and the mapping of *virtual networks onto substrate resources*.

---

## Research Areas
Ammar's research portfolio covers a broad range of networking topics, with recurring themes that connect theory, algorithms, and practical systems. He advanced the study of connectivity in sparse and intermittently connected wireless networks through the message-ferrying paradigm ("A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks", 2004; "Message ferry route design for sparse ad hoc networks with mobile nodes", 2006) and through control and routing techniques for predictable and controlled mobility ("Controlling the mobility of multiple data transport ferries in a delay-tolerant network", 2005; "Routing in space and time in networks with predictable mobility", 2004). In the multimedia and multicast domain he produced influential work on scalable video-on-demand and layered multicast schemes ("The use of multicast delivery to provide a scalable and interactive video-on-demand service", 2002; "Layered video multicast with retransmissions (LVMR)", 1997/1998), and on multicast security and group behavior. Ammar has also contributed to the emergence of *network virtualization* and *NFV* through algorithms for embedding virtual network requests onto physical substrates ("Algorithms for Assigning Substrate Network Resources to Virtual Network Components.", 2006) and service function chain placement and migration research (2017). Other strands of his work include reputation and incentive mechanisms for P2P systems ("A reputation system for peer-to-peer networks", 2003), edge and device-based cloud concepts ("Femto clouds: Leveraging mobile devices to provide cloud service at the edge", 2015; "Cosmos: computation offloading as a service for mobile devices", 2014), measurement and privacy for network traces (prefix-preserving IP anonymization), and scalable simulation and evaluation methods for large-scale networks.

## Notable Works
- "A message ferrying approach for data delivery in sparse mobile ad hoc networks" (2004) — introduced the message-ferrying paradigm for providing timely and efficient data delivery in *sparsely connected MANETs/DTNs*, forming a foundational line of work on mobile relays and controlled mobility that has influenced both theory and system designs (highly cited and followed by route-design and control extensions).
- "Algorithms for Assigning Substrate Network Resources to Virtual Network Components." (2006) — a seminal contribution to *network virtualization* that formalized the substrate-to-virtual mapping problem and proposed algorithmic approaches widely cited in virtual network embedding and NFV research.
- "A reputation system for peer-to-peer networks" (2003) — proposed a practical reputation framework for decentralized P2P systems, shaping subsequent work on incentives, trust, and secure peer selection in unstructured overlay networks.

## Academic Background
Mostafa Ammar has had a long and distinguished academic career at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, holding the title of Regents' Professor and serving in senior leadership as Interim Chair of the School of Computer Science. Over decades of research he has authored and coauthored many highly cited papers (total citations approximately 24,887) and patents spanning networking protocols, distributed systems, and content distribution. His scholarship bridges theoretical models, algorithm design, and experimental systems, and has informed both academic research agendas and practical deployments in DTNs, multicast systems, virtualization/NFV, and mobile/cloud offloading. He maintains an active research group at Georgia Tech (http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~ammar), has supervised numerous students and postdocs, and has collaborated broadly across industry and academia to translate research into standards, systems, and patents.
